core.when: When refactoring the pyliferisk module structure
core.action: Merge MortalityTable and Actuarial classes into a single monolithic class — these must remain separate to enable reuse of mortality data with different interest rates

consequence.description: Consolidating the two classes couples mortality calculations with interest-based commutations, eliminating the ability to reuse mortality tables across different interest rate scenarios for sensitivity testing

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-003

core.when: When implementing the Actuarial class constructor
core.action: Compute commutation functions (Dx, Cx, Mx, Nx) eagerly in __init__ — do not implement lazy computation where these values are calculated on-demand during formula access

consequence.description: Lazy computation of commutation functions adds computational overhead to each formula call, causing significant performance degradation during batch premium and reserve calculations

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-005

core.when: When implementing mortality rate calculations
core.action: Store mortality rates in per-mille convention (qx*1000) and maintain this format throughout calculations — do not normalize to decimal representation (0-1 scale)

consequence.description: Converting qx from per-mille to decimal representation introduces systematic factor-of-1000 errors in all premium and reserve calculations, causing severe mispricing in insurance products

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-001

validation_threshold: qx_value < 1 AND qx_value > 0 → FAIL (per-mille convention violated)

core.when: When constructing MortalityTable from base table data
core.action: Initialize lx[0] to 100000 (radix convention) — do not change to alternative values like 1,000,000 or 1

consequence.description: Using a non-standard radix value breaks compatibility with industry-standard actuarial references and requires recalibration of all premium formulas, causing backtest-live inconsistency

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-002

core.when: When calculating the Dx commutation function (discounted survivors)
core.action: Compute Dx as ((1/(1+i))^age) * lx[x] — Dx represents present value at policy inception of survivors at age x, discounted at interest rate i for the full policy year

consequence.description: Incorrect Dx calculation using mid-year discounting or wrong exponent introduces pricing errors in all annuity, endowment, and level premium calculations based on the equivalence principle

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-020

validation_threshold: Dx formula not equal to ((1/(1+i))^age) * lx[x] → FAIL

core.when: When calculating the Nx commutation function (cumulative discounted survivors)
core.action: Compute Nx as the cumulative sum of Dx from age x to the limiting age — Nx represents the total present value of a unit lifetime annuity beginning at age x

consequence.description: Incorrect Nx calculation (recalculating Dx sums per age, or using partial range) causes pricing errors in whole-life and term annuity calculations, affecting net premium denominators

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-022

validation_threshold: Nx != sum(Dx[age:] for all ages to limiting_age) → FAIL

core.when: When calculating insurance reserves for regulatory reporting
core.action: Calculate reserve using prospective method: Reserve = APV(Future Premiums) - APV(Future Claims) — reserve represents insurer's net liability to policyholder

consequence.description: Using retrospective reserve calculation or alternative methods violates insurance regulatory requirements for reserve reporting, potentially causing regulatory penalties and insufficient fund allocation

evidence_refs.summary: Derived from BD: BD-031

core.action: Verify that the perc parameter (percentage loading) matches the insurer's actual portfolio experience before applying to premium calculations — default loading may not reflect actual vs expected mortality

consequence.description: Using default perc parameter without verification causes pricing inaccuracy when actual portfolio mortality differs from standard table assumptions, leading to either insufficient reserves or uncompetitive pricing

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-004

core.action: Create a new Actuarial object with transformed interest rate j = (i - qx) / (1 + qx) for qAx calculations — do not use in-place modification of the original interest rate

consequence.description: Using the original interest rate i instead of the transformed rate j = (i-q)/(1+q) for qAx calculations produces incorrect pricing for deferred and increasing benefit products, systematically misvaluing policy reserves

core.when: When implementing geometric progression annuity calculations in actuarial models
core.action: Use the effective rate formula j = (i - q) / (1 + q) where i is the nominal interest rate and q is the geometric progression parameter; verify both rates are expressed as effective rates per payment period

consequence.description: Incorrect annuity rate calculation propagates to premium calculations, causing either overcharging or undercharging policyholders; pricing errors lead to financial losses or competitive disadvantage

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-076

core.when: When implementing mortality loading adjustment in actuarial calculations
core.action: Apply mortality loading where perc=100 represents exactly 100% of base mortality rates (no adjustment); values greater than 100 increase mortality rates (for select/standard tables) and values less than 100 decrease mortality rates (for preferred lives)

consequence.description: Incorrect loading interpretation causes systematic over or underestimation of mortality rates, leading to mispriced premiums that result in either insurer losses or uncompetitive pricing

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-079

core.when: When modifying or refactoring commutation function calculations (Dx, Mx, Cx) in insurance premium formulas
core.action: Modify Dx computation (discount factor v^age and lx[x] dependent) or Mx computation (Cx dependent) without validating impact on Ax = Mx[x]/Dx[x] formula; any change to commutation functions requires regression testing of each downstream premium calculations

consequence.description: Silent propagation of errors through the hidden dependency chain Ax ← Mx ← Cx and Ax ← Dx ← lx[x],v; incorrect premiums may go undetected until financial losses materialize in claims settlements

evidence_refs.summary: 5c28ca3 Derived from BD: BD-083

core.when: When loading mortality tables into actuarial calculation systems
core.action: Map table index 0 to the starting age as specified by the mortality table source (e.g., CSO tables typically start at age 0 or 18 depending on table type); verify alignment between table data array indexing and the declared starting age before any calculation

consequence.description: Misaligned starting age causes all mortality rates to be applied to wrong ages, producing incorrect premiums and reserves that compound over the policy term; error affects every policy using the table
